exp_L1_00.label: Diagnostic Accuracy & Reliability
exp_L1_00.description: Concerns about whether AI tools actually help us make better, safer diagnoses—or if they introduce new errors or blind spots.

exp_L2_00_00.label: False Positives/Negatives
exp_L2_00_00.description: AI systems that cry wolf too often or miss the real emergencies.

exp_leaf_00_00_00.label: Too many false alarms on incidental findings
exp_leaf_00_00_00.description: The AI flags every tiny lung nodule as suspicious—even the ones we know are benign based on years of follow-up. It’s like having a nervous intern who panics over coffee stains on the film.

exp_leaf_00_00_01.label: Misses subtle but critical pathologies
exp_leaf_00_00_01.description: It’ll breeze past an early subdural hematoma or a tiny pneumothorax because it’s trained mostly on textbook cases, not the messy reality we see at 2 a.m. in the ER.

exp_leaf_00_00_02.label: Overconfident wrong answers
exp_leaf_00_00_02.description: The model gives a 98% confidence score on something that’s clearly wrong—like calling a rib fracture a metastasis—and it’s hard to override because the UI makes it look authoritative.

exp_leaf_00_00_03.label: Struggles with rare diseases
exp_leaf_00_00_03.description: Good luck getting it to recognize sarcoidosis or Erdheim-Chester disease—it’s never seen enough cases, and it defaults to ‘normal’ or ‘common mimic.’

exp_L2_00_01.label: Generalization Across Populations
exp_L2_00_01.description: AI trained on one hospital’s data doesn’t work well when you move it to another—or on patients who don’t fit the training mold.

exp_leaf_00_01_00.label: Poor performance on underrepresented demographics
exp_leaf_00_01_00.description: It’s great at spotting breast cancer in white women over 50, but misses denser patterns in younger Black or Asian patients because those weren’t well represented in the training set.

exp_leaf_00_01_01.label: Fails with different scanner protocols
exp_leaf_00_01_01.description: Switch from a Siemens to a GE scanner, or tweak the slice thickness, and suddenly the AI starts hallucinating artifacts or missing lesions—it’s brittle as glass.

exp_leaf_00_01_02.label: Can’t handle poor image quality
exp_leaf_00_01_02.description: In trauma or ICU settings, images are often motion-blurred or low-dose—but the AI was trained on pristine outpatient scans, so it either crashes or gives nonsense.

exp_leaf_00_01_03.label: Misinterprets post-op or post-radiation changes
exp_leaf_00_01_03.description: It sees surgical clips or fibrosis and screams ‘recurrence!’—doesn’t understand the clinical timeline or prior interventions.

exp_L2_00_02.label: Explainability & Trust
exp_L2_00_02.description: We can’t act on AI suggestions if we don’t understand why it made them—especially when stakes are high.

exp_leaf_00_02_00.label: Heatmaps don’t match radiologist reasoning
exp_leaf_00_02_00.description: The AI highlights a spot on the liver, but I know that’s just a beam-hardening artifact—the heatmap looks convincing but is pointing at noise.

exp_leaf_00_02_01.label: No way to interrogate the ‘why’
exp_leaf_00_02_01.description: When it says ‘likely malignant,’ I can’t ask: ‘Compared to what? Which features tipped you off?’ It’s a black box with a pretty UI.

exp_leaf_00_02_02.label: Hard to calibrate trust over time
exp_leaf_00_02_02.description: At first you’re skeptical, then it gets a few right and you start leaning on it—until it blindsides you with a catastrophic miss. There’s no intuitive way to know when to doubt it.

exp_leaf_00_02_03.label: Can’t integrate with differential diagnosis thinking
exp_leaf_00_02_03.description: I’m weighing five possibilities based on history, labs, and imaging—but the AI just spits out one label. It doesn’t play well with clinical reasoning.

exp_L1_01.label: Workflow Integration & Efficiency
exp_L1_01.description: How AI fits—or doesn’t—into our already overloaded, high-stakes daily routines.

exp_L2_01_00.label: Disruption of Reading Flow
exp_L2_01_00.description: AI tools that slow us down or force awkward detours in our mental process.

exp_leaf_01_00_00.label: Forces context switching between systems
exp_leaf_01_00_00.description: I have to click out of PACS into some clunky AI portal, wait for loading, then click back—kills my rhythm during a 200-study night shift.

exp_leaf_01_00_01.label: Adds steps without saving time
exp_leaf_01_00_01.description: It promises to save 10 minutes per case, but then I spend 12 minutes verifying its output or correcting its markup. Net loss.

exp_leaf_01_00_02.label: Interrupts deep focus during complex reads
exp_leaf_01_00_02.description: When I’m deep in a neuro MRI trying to trace a subtle MS plaque, a pop-up saying ‘lung nodule detected’ yanks me out of the zone.

exp_leaf_01_00_03.label: Poor handling of multi-series studies
exp_leaf_01_00_03.description: It analyzes each series in isolation—so it might flag a ‘new lesion’ on post-contrast T1 that was already visible on pre-contrast, because it didn’t fuse the sequences.

exp_L2_01_01.label: Prioritization & Triage Failures
exp_L2_01_01.description: AI that misjudges urgency or floods us with low-yield alerts.

exp_leaf_01_01_00.label: Over-prioritizes trivial findings
exp_leaf_01_01_00.description: Flags a stable 3mm lung nodule as ‘critical’ while missing a growing adrenal mass that’s actually urgent—gets the clinical stakes completely backwards.

exp_leaf_01_01_01.label: Underestimates time-sensitive emergencies
exp_leaf_01_01_01.description: In stroke cases, it takes 90 seconds to process—by then, the window for thrombectomy has narrowed. Speed matters more than precision here.

exp_leaf_01_01_02.label: Can’t adapt to service-specific priorities
exp_leaf_01_01_02.description: In screening mammo, we want high sensitivity; in diagnostic MRI, we need specificity. But the AI uses the same threshold everywhere.

exp_leaf_01_01_03.label: Generates redundant alerts for known issues
exp_leaf_01_01_03.description: Keeps flagging a chronic cyst as ‘new’ because it doesn’t link properly to prior exams or ignores dictated stability notes.

exp_L2_01_02.label: User Interface & Interaction Design
exp_L2_01_02.description: Clunky, unintuitive, or non-customizable interfaces that feel like they were designed by engineers who’ve never read a scan.

exp_leaf_01_02_00.label: Non-adjustable thresholds or filters
exp_leaf_01_02_00.description: I can’t tell it to ‘only show me things bigger than 6mm’ or ‘ignore spine degeneration’—it’s all or nothing.

exp_leaf_01_02_01.label: Poor integration with voice recognition
exp_leaf_01_02_01.description: I dictate ‘no acute abnormality,’ but the AI insists on inserting its own structured findings that I then have to delete—wastes more time than it saves.

exp_leaf_01_02_02.label: Lacks batch or bulk actions
exp_leaf_01_02_02.description: If it flags 10 stable nodules across 5 prior studies, I have to dismiss each one individually—no ‘dismiss all similar’ button.

exp_leaf_01_02_03.label: Visual markup clashes with my own annotations
exp_leaf_01_02_03.description: Its bounding boxes overlap my arrows or measurements, making the image unreadable—feels like someone scribbling over my notes.

exp_L1_02.label: Legal, Ethical & Regulatory Risks
exp_L1_02.description: Liability, compliance, and moral dilemmas introduced or amplified by AI use.

exp_L2_02_00.label: Liability Ambiguity
exp_L2_02_00.description: Who’s responsible when AI contributes to a missed diagnosis?

exp_leaf_02_00_00.label: Unclear medico-legal responsibility
exp_leaf_02_00_00.description: If I rely on AI and it misses a bleed, am I liable for not catching it—or is the vendor? The law hasn’t caught up, and my malpractice insurer is vague.

exp_leaf_02_00_01.label: Pressure to use AI even when skeptical
exp_leaf_02_00_01.description: Administration says ‘you must use the AI tool’—but if I override it and something goes wrong, I’m on the hook for not trusting the ‘standard of care.’

exp_leaf_02_00_02.label: Documentation burden increases
exp_leaf_02_00_02.description: Now I have to explicitly state in every report whether I agreed or disagreed with the AI—adds verbiage and legal exposure.

exp_leaf_02_00_03.label: Fear of being second-guessed by non-radiologists
exp_leaf_02_00_03.description: A hospital exec sees the AI flagged something I dismissed, and questions my competence—even though I had solid clinical rationale.

exp_L2_02_01.label: Regulatory & Compliance Burden
exp_L2_02_01.description: Keeping up with evolving FDA rules, hospital policies, and data governance requirements.

exp_leaf_02_01_00.label: Frequent revalidation requirements
exp_leaf_02_01_00.description: Every time the vendor updates the model, we have to re-test it locally—which takes weeks of phantom studies and committee approvals.

exp_leaf_02_01_01.label: Unclear FDA clearance scope
exp_leaf_02_01_01.description: The tool is cleared for ‘lung nodule detection’—but is using it off-label for thyroid nodules on neck CTs a regulatory violation?

exp_leaf_02_01_02.label: Data residency and privacy conflicts
exp_leaf_02_01_02.description: The AI cloud processes images offshore, but our IRB requires all patient data to stay on-prem—forces us to choose between innovation and compliance.

exp_leaf_02_01_03.label: Inconsistent institutional policies
exp_leaf_02_01_03.description: One hospital allows AI-assisted reporting; the next-door affiliate bans it. Creates chaos for teleradiology groups like ours.

exp_L2_02_02.label: Ethical Dilemmas
exp_L2_02_02.description: Moral tensions around autonomy, equity, and transparency.

exp_leaf_02_02_00.label: Patient consent for AI use is murky
exp_leaf_02_02_00.description: Do we need to tell patients their scan was ‘read with AI help’? Most places don’t, but ethically, shouldn’t they know?

exp_leaf_02_02_01.label: Reinforces health disparities
exp_leaf_02_02_01.description: If the AI works worse on minority populations, and we deploy it widely, we’re baking inequity into the system at scale.

exp_leaf_02_02_02.label: Erodes trainee learning
exp_leaf_02_02_02.description: Residents lean on AI instead of developing their own pattern recognition—creates a generation that can’t read without a digital crutch.

exp_leaf_02_02_03.label: Commercial influence on clinical decisions
exp_leaf_02_02_03.description: Vendors push tools that highlight billable findings—subtly steering us toward more follow-ups, more scans, more revenue.

exp_L1_03.label: Team Dynamics & Communication
exp_L1_03.description: How AI affects collaboration between radiologists, technologists, referrers, and patients.

exp_L2_03_00.label: Referring Physician Misunderstandings
exp_L2_03_00.description: Clinicians misinterpret or over-rely on AI outputs without understanding limitations.

exp_leaf_03_00_00.label: Clinicians treat AI findings as definitive
exp_leaf_03_00_00.description: They see ‘AI-detected nodule’ in the report and order a PET scan immediately—without waiting for my contextual interpretation.

exp_leaf_03_00_01.label: Increased consult volume for AI-flagged benign findings
exp_leaf_03_00_01.description: Primary care docs call asking about ‘that AI finding’—which I already dismissed as artifact—but now I have to explain it all over again.

exp_leaf_03_00_02.label: Blame shifting when AI disagrees with clinician
exp_leaf_03_00_02.description: If the AI says ‘no fracture’ but the ortho doc is sure there’s one, they accuse us of hiding behind the algorithm.

exp_leaf_03_00_03.label: Poor communication of AI uncertainty
exp_leaf_03_00_03.description: We say ‘low suspicion,’ but the EHR displays it as a binary ‘detected/not detected’—so nuance gets lost in translation.

exp_L2_03_01.label: Radiologist-Technologist Coordination
exp_L2_03_01.description: AI introduces new handoff points and potential misalignments with imaging techs.

exp_leaf_03_01_00.label: Techs adjust protocols based on AI prompts
exp_leaf_03_01_00.description: They add extra sequences because the AI ‘requested’ them—but it’s not calibrated for our workflow, leading to longer scan times and backlog.

exp_leaf_03_01_01.label: QA responsibilities become blurred
exp_leaf_03_01_01.description: If AI auto-rejects a study for motion, is it the tech’s fault or the algorithm’s? No clear ownership.

exp_leaf_03_01_02.label: Techs lose situational awareness
exp_leaf_03_01_02.description: They assume AI will catch positioning errors, so they’re less meticulous—degrading overall image quality upstream.

exp_L2_03_02.label: Inter-Radiologist Consistency
exp_L2_03_02.description: AI creates divergence in how different radiologists use or interpret its output.

exp_leaf_03_02_00.label: Variable adoption across experience levels
exp_leaf_03_02_00.description: Junior radiologists follow AI suggestions closely; seniors ignore them—leads to inconsistent reports from the same group.

exp_leaf_03_02_01.label: Disagreements over AI overrides
exp_leaf_03_02_01.description: Two partners review the same case—one trusts the AI, the other overrides it—and now there’s internal conflict over ‘correct’ interpretation.

exp_leaf_03_02_02.label: Night float vs. day team misalignment
exp_leaf_03_02_02.description: The overnight radiologist uses AI to triage, but the daytime team doesn’t trust those flags—causing delays and duplicated work.

exp_leaf_03_02_03.label: Subspecialty resistance
exp_leaf_03_02_03.description: Neuroradiologists reject chest AI tools, body imagers hate neuro AI—creates silos and resentment within the department.

exp_L1_04.label: Economic & Operational Pressures
exp_L1_04.description: Financial, staffing, and resource constraints that shape how AI is adopted—or rejected.

exp_L2_04_00.label: Cost vs. Value Uncertainty
exp_L2_04_00.description: Hard to justify AI expenses when ROI is nebulous or delayed.

exp_leaf_04_00_00.label: High licensing fees with unclear productivity gains
exp_leaf_04_00_00.description: We pay $150K/year per module, but studies show only marginal time savings—hard to defend to hospital CFOs.

exp_leaf_04_00_01.label: Hidden integration costs
exp_leaf_04_00_01.description: Nobody told us we’d need a dedicated IT person just to keep the AI pipeline running—now we’re burning budget on support staff.

exp_leaf_04_00_02.label: Vendor lock-in risks
exp_leaf_04_00_02.description: Once you build workflows around one vendor’s AI, switching becomes prohibitively expensive—even if a better tool emerges.

exp_leaf_04_00_03.label: Reimbursement doesn’t cover AI use
exp_leaf_04_00_03.description: We can’t bill for ‘AI-assisted interpretation,’ so all the cost comes out of our margin—makes adoption financially irrational.

exp_L2_04_01.label: Staffing & Training Gaps
exp_L2_04_01.description: Lack of personnel skilled in both radiology and AI operations.

exp_leaf_04_01_00.label: No in-house AI champions
exp_leaf_04_01_00.description: We bought the tool, but nobody knows how to optimize thresholds, interpret logs, or troubleshoot failures—so it sits half-used.

exp_leaf_04_01_01.label: Training materials are vendor-biased
exp_leaf_04_01_01.description: All the tutorials come from the seller—they teach you how to love the product, not how to critically evaluate its failures.

exp_leaf_04_01_02.label: Burnout from constant adaptation
exp_leaf_04_01_02.description: Just as we get used to one AI, another rolls out—feels like we’re always relearning instead of practicing medicine.

exp_leaf_04_01_03.label: Difficulty evaluating competing tools
exp_leaf_04_01_03.description: Vendors give demo datasets cherry-picked to show success—we have no independent way to compare real-world performance.

exp_L2_04_02.label: Scalability & Infrastructure Limits
exp_L2_04_02.description: Technical bottlenecks that prevent smooth AI deployment at scale.

exp_leaf_04_02_00.label: GPU shortages delay processing
exp_leaf_04_02_00.description: During peak hours, AI jobs queue up—so emergency scans sit waiting while the system crunches routine mammograms.

exp_leaf_04_02_01.label: DICOM compatibility issues
exp_leaf_04_02_01.description: Older modalities send non-standard headers, and the AI chokes—forcing manual intervention or study rejection.

exp_leaf_04_02_02.label: Storage bloat from AI metadata
exp_leaf_04_02_02.description: Every study now carries 50MB of AI heatmaps and logs—our archive is filling up faster than projected.

exp_leaf_04_02_03.label: Network latency in distributed sites
exp_leaf_04_02_03.description: Rural clinics upload studies to a central AI server—takes 10 minutes just to transfer, negating any speed benefit.

res_L1_00.label: Model Interpretability & Trust
res_L1_00.description: Understanding how domain experts assess and build trust in AI-generated predictions from virtual cell models.

res_L2_00_00.label: Reliability Assessment
res_L2_00_00.description: How experts evaluate whether a virtual cell model is trustworthy or reliable.

res_leaf_00_00_00.label: Identify metrics used to judge model reliability
res_leaf_00_00_00.description: Determine which quantitative or qualitative metrics (e.g., accuracy, biological plausibility, reproducibility) influence experts’ trust in virtual cell models like CellForge.

res_leaf_00_00_01.label: Assess role of domain knowledge in reliability judgments
res_leaf_00_00_01.description: Explore how experts integrate prior biological knowledge when evaluating whether a model’s output is credible.

res_leaf_00_00_02.label: Understand thresholds for acceptable uncertainty
res_leaf_00_00_02.description: Investigate what levels of prediction uncertainty or error experts tolerate in different diagnostic or research contexts.

res_L2_00_01.label: Explainability Needs
res_L2_00_01.description: What kinds of explanations do experts need to understand AI predictions in gene perturbation tasks?

res_leaf_00_01_00.label: Determine desired causal explanations
res_leaf_00_01_00.description: Identify whether experts seek mechanistic, counterfactual, or associative explanations for why a gene perturbation leads to specific downstream effects.

res_leaf_00_01_01.label: Map explanation types to scientific goals
res_leaf_00_01_01.description: Link specific explanation needs (e.g., gene-gene influence, trait-level impact) to broader research objectives like drug discovery or disease mechanism elucidation.

res_leaf_00_01_02.label: Evaluate sufficiency of current XAI methods
res_leaf_00_01_02.description: Assess whether existing explainable AI techniques (e.g., saliency maps, feature importance) meet the explanatory depth required by domain experts.

res_L1_01.label: User Workflow Integration
res_L1_01.description: How AI-assisted tools fit into or disrupt existing scientific workflows in genetic perturbation research.

res_L2_01_00.label: Current Experimental Practices
res_L2_01_00.description: Document manual workflows for genetic perturbation to identify integration points for AI assistance.

res_leaf_01_00_00.label: Characterize manual gene perturbation workflow
res_leaf_01_00_00.description: Elicit step-by-step descriptions of how experts currently design, execute, and interpret genetic perturbation experiments without AI.

res_leaf_01_00_01.label: Identify bottlenecks amenable to AI support
res_leaf_01_00_01.description: Pinpoint time-consuming, error-prone, or cognitively demanding steps where virtual cell models could provide value.

res_leaf_01_00_02.label: Understand data provenance expectations
res_leaf_01_00_02.description: Determine how experts track experimental conditions, controls, and assumptions—and how AI tools must support this traceability.

res_L2_01_01.label: AI Tool Adoption Barriers
res_L2_01_01.description: Explore challenges that prevent or complicate the use of AI-based gene prediction models in practice.

res_leaf_01_01_00.label: Catalog usability and technical barriers
res_leaf_01_01_00.description: Identify interface, computational, or interoperability issues that hinder adoption of tools like CellForge.

res_leaf_01_01_01.label: Assess mismatch between model outputs and user needs
res_leaf_01_01_01.description: Investigate whether AI predictions are presented in formats or granularities that align with expert decision-making processes.

res_leaf_01_01_02.label: Understand resistance due to epistemic norms
res_leaf_01_01_02.description: Explore whether cultural or disciplinary norms in biology discourage reliance on black-box AI predictions.

res_L1_02.label: Knowledge Discovery & Scientific Insight
res_L1_02.description: How virtual cell models can support novel biological insight generation beyond prediction accuracy.

res_L2_02_00.label: Desired Knowledge Outcomes
res_L2_02_00.description: Clarify what kinds of scientific understanding experts aim to gain through AI-assisted perturbation.

res_leaf_02_00_00.label: Prioritize types of biological insights sought
res_leaf_02_00_00.description: Rank the importance of goals such as identifying regulatory gene networks, predicting phenotypic outcomes, or discovering therapeutic targets.

res_leaf_02_00_01.label: Distinguish hypothesis generation vs. validation needs
res_leaf_02_00_01.description: Determine whether experts primarily use AI models to generate new hypotheses or to test existing ones.

res_leaf_02_00_02.label: Assess need for multi-scale explanations
res_leaf_02_00_02.description: Explore whether experts require explanations that connect molecular changes (gene expression) to cellular or organism-level traits.

res_L2_02_01.label: Supporting Evidence Integration
res_L2_02_01.description: How external knowledge sources can enhance interpretation of AI predictions.

res_leaf_02_01_00.label: Identify trusted external data sources
res_leaf_02_01_00.description: Determine which types of evidence (e.g., literature, pathway databases, prior experiments) experts rely on to contextualize AI outputs.

res_leaf_02_01_01.label: Design for literature-model alignment
res_leaf_02_01_01.description: Explore how visual or interactive linking between model predictions and published findings could improve credibility and insight.

res_leaf_02_01_02.label: Understand role of negative evidence
res_leaf_02_01_02.description: Investigate how experts use known non-effects or failed experiments to calibrate trust in AI predictions.

res_L1_03.label: Expert-AI Collaboration Design
res_L1_03.description: How to design interfaces and interactions that support effective collaboration between biologists and AI systems.

res_L2_03_00.label: Interactive Explanation Preferences
res_L2_03_00.description: What interaction modalities best support expert understanding of AI behavior under perturbation.

res_leaf_03_00_00.label: Evaluate effectiveness of proposed XAI methods
res_leaf_03_00_00.description: Assess expert receptivity to specific explainability techniques (e.g., attention maps, perturbation heatmaps) introduced in supporting materials.

res_leaf_03_00_01.label: Determine need for what-if exploration
res_leaf_03_00_01.description: Investigate whether experts want to interactively test alternative perturbations and observe predicted outcomes in real time.

res_leaf_03_00_02.label: Assess preference for comparative views
res_leaf_03_00_02.description: Explore whether side-by-side comparisons (e.g., wild-type vs. perturbed, model vs. literature) aid interpretation.

res_L2_03_01.label: Cognitive & Epistemic Support
res_L2_03_01.description: How system design can reduce cognitive burden and support scientific reasoning.

res_leaf_03_01_00.label: Identify cognitive overload triggers
res_leaf_03_01_00.description: Pinpoint aspects of AI output (e.g., dimensionality, uncertainty representation) that overwhelm or confuse experts.

res_leaf_03_01_01.label: Support mental model construction
res_leaf_03_01_01.description: Determine how visualizations or interactions can help experts build accurate internal models of the AI’s behavior and limitations.

res_leaf_03_01_02.label: Enable confidence calibration
res_leaf_03_01_02.description: Explore how to communicate model confidence in ways that help experts appropriately trust or question predictions.

aligned_path_researcher: Identify metrics used to judge model reliability

conflict.expert_branch: Too many false alarms on incidental findings

assistance_payload.validate_focus: Your focus on reducing false alarms from incidental findings is critically important—overcalling benign nodules not only causes unnecessary patient anxiety but also burdens clinical workflows with redundant follow-ups.

pivot.limitation: Relying solely on observed false alarm rates without a standardized framework for reliability can make it difficult to distinguish between model flaws, data artifacts, or true clinical uncertainty—especially when deploying AI across diverse patient populations.
pivot.research_goal: Identify metrics used to judge model reliability, such as biological plausibility and reproducibility, to ground trust in consistent, interpretable criteria rather than anecdotal performance.
pivot.compelling_reason: By anchoring reliability assessments in validated metrics, you gain a proactive way to filter out models prone to overcalling before they reach your clinic—preserving your time and protecting patients from unnecessary interventions.
pivot.coarse_scenario: In a typical morning, you review a lung scan flagged by an AI system that’s already been vetted using reliability metrics aligned with clinical reality. Instead of chasing phantom nodules, you confidently focus on the few truly concerning findings—knowing the system has been calibrated to ignore the noise you’ve spent years learning to disregard.

aligned_path_researcher: Assess role of domain knowledge in reliability judgments

conflict.researcher_branch: Assess role of domain knowledge in reliability judgments

structural_mapping.inputs: An ambiguous or atypical imaging finding (e.g., a rib fracture with unusual callus formation) that falls outside the model’s high-confidence training distribution
structural_mapping.logic: The AI applies pattern-matching based on dominant training examples, assigns high confidence to an incorrect label, and presents it with interface cues that discourage clinician override—mirroring how overconfidence masks uncertainty
structural_mapping.outputs: A misleadingly certain diagnosis that contradicts clinical intuition but is hard to dismiss due to the system’s authoritative presentation

analogy.explanation: Just as the expert distrusts overconfident AI outputs that ignore contextual plausibility, the researcher’s focus on domain knowledge in reliability judgments examines how clinicians use their understanding of disease mechanisms and imaging artifacts to question or validate such outputs—even when the model appears statistically confident. Both hinge on the mismatch between algorithmic certainty and clinical reasonableness.

scenario.inputs: A non-contrast head CT from a trauma patient showing a faint, crescent-shaped hyperdensity along the right frontal convexity—subtle enough that it could be artifact or early subdural hematoma—and the AI model outputs 'no acute intracranial hemorrhage' with 94% confidence
scenario.outputs: The researcher’s approach would document how the radiologist uses knowledge of trauma biomechanics, typical hematoma appearance timelines, and common beam-hardening artifacts to judge the AI’s output as unreliable despite its high confidence, then override it based on that domain-informed skepticism
scenario.edge_cases: When the pathology is both subtle and rare (e.g., a small posterior fossa subdural in a child), the expert may miss it too—but the researcher’s framework focuses not on the miss itself, but on whether the expert’s domain knowledge was actively engaged to assess the AI’s reliability, even if the final judgment is still incorrect

aligned_path_researcher: Assess role of domain knowledge in reliability judgments

conflict.researcher_branch: Assess role of domain knowledge in reliability judgments

assistance_payload.validate_focus: Your focus on poor performance in underrepresented demographics rightly highlights a critical real-world risk—AI systems that work well for some patients but fail others due to biased training data can worsen health disparities.

pivot.limitation: Relying solely on performance metrics across demographic groups may not reveal why the model fails or how to distinguish true biological signals from data artifacts, especially when ground truth is uncertain or sparse.
pivot.research_goal: Assess how domain knowledge—like known biological patterns in tissue density or disease progression—can inform more reliable judgments about when the AI’s output is trustworthy.
pivot.compelling_reason: By integrating clinician expertise into reliability assessments, you gain a proactive safeguard against misdiagnosis in underrepresented groups, turning subjective insight into a structured check against algorithmic blind spots.
pivot.coarse_scenario: On a typical day, a radiologist reviewing an AI-flagged scan for a young Black patient pauses when the result seems inconsistent with known biological patterns. Drawing on integrated domain knowledge, they override a false negative, catch an early tumor, and log the case to improve future model behavior—building both trust and equity simultaneously.

scenario.inputs: A radiologist uses an AI tool to flag a lung nodule on a CT scan. The AI was trained on scans from Site A using Protocol X, but the current scan came from Site B using Protocol Y (different slice thickness and contrast timing). The radiologist doesn’t know this mismatch occurred.
scenario.outputs: The researcher’s approach would require the AI system to automatically log and display: (1) training data provenance (Site A, Protocol X), (2) input scan metadata (Site B, Protocol Y), and (3) a warning flag about protocol mismatch—enabling the expert to contextualize the AI’s confidence.
scenario.edge_cases: If the hospital’s PACS strips metadata during ingestion (a common integration gap), the AI tool receives no protocol info—making provenance tracking impossible unless IT infrastructure is modified. The expert sees this as another hidden integration cost; the researcher sees it as a failure to meet basic data expectations.
